During the pre-flight walkround as a matter of routine
Only during the annual airworthiness review

q: What maintenance is required after work on the bleed air supply or piccolo tube system?
opts: Borescope inspection of the piccolo tubes
A leak check and functional test
A visual inspection of the leading edge only
An NDT inspection of the piccolo tube welds

q: How are the piccolo tubes distributed along the leading edge?
opts: They are connected at a single central point
They run along the leading edge with multiple small holes to impinge hot air on the inner surface
They are located externally on the lower surface of the slat
They use high-pressure nozzles to direct air at the leading edge tip

q: What are the "Dirty Dozen" in aviation maintenance?
opts: Twelve common defect types found on aircraft
Twelve common human conditions that can lead to maintenance errors
Twelve prohibited maintenance practices
Twelve critical components that require dual inspection

q: Why is fatigue particularly dangerous in maintenance?
opts: Fatigue causes technicians to rush and skip steps
Fatigue reduces concentration, slows reaction times, and impairs decision-making
Fatigue increases physical strength, leading to overtorque of fasteners
Fatigue reduces stress levels, leading to complacency

q: What is a "just culture" in aviation maintenance organisations?
opts: A culture where all errors are punished to deter future mistakes
A culture that encourages error reporting without fear of punishment to improve safety
A culture that blames individuals for systemic failures
A culture where only management decisions are questioned

q: What is the purpose of post-maintenance inspections by a second inspector?
opts: To verify the quality of the documentation
A critical control to catch errors, especially on critical systems
To ensure tools are accounted for
To check the inspector's licence is current

q: Which of the following is NOT one of the "Dirty Dozen"?
opts: Fatigue
Distraction
Corrosion
Complacency

q: How can water enter aircraft fuel tanks?
opts: Only through the refuelling connection
Through condensation, poorly sealed filler caps, or contaminated refuelling equipment
Through the fuel vent system during high-humidity conditions
Through leaking crossfeed valves

q: What does microbiological contamination in fuel tanks produce?
opts: Excess water and ice crystals
Acids, dark sediment, and corrosion to tank structure and components
Increased fuel viscosity
Explosive vapour that accumulates in the ullage

q: What is the purpose of a tank sump check?
opts: To measure the fuel quantity accurately
To drain the lowest point of the tank and inspect the sample for water and debris
To pressurise the tank for leak testing
To aerate the fuel before fuelling operations

q: What can be used to control microbial growth in fuel tanks?
opts: Nitrogen inerting of the tank
Biocide additives
UV light treatment of the fuel
Anti-static treatments applied to the tank walls

q: Why is fuel contamination by incorrect fuel type extremely serious?
opts: Incorrect fuel changes the weight of the aircraft
It can cause engine damage or failure
It invalidates the aircraft's airworthiness certificate immediately
It causes microbiological growth to accelerate

q: Which NDT method is most effective for detecting corrosion under aircraft skin?
opts: Dye penetrant inspection
Magnetic particle inspection
Eddy current testing
X-ray testing

q: What type of materials can be inspected with Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)?
opts: All metals including aluminium and titanium
Only ferrous (magnetic) metals
Only non-metallic composite materials
Any conductive material

q: In dye penetrant inspection, what is the purpose of the developer?
opts: To clean the surface before applying penetrant
To draw the penetrant out of cracks, making them visible
To remove the excess penetrant from the surface
To increase the surface porosity for better penetrant absorption

q: What does ultrasonic testing detect?
opts: Surface corrosion on metal surfaces
Defects that reflect high-frequency sound waves
Cracks in composite materials only
Magnetic field irregularities around cracks

q: Why is X-ray testing used for complex assemblies?
opts: It is the fastest and cheapest NDT method
It creates an image of the internal structure and can access areas where other methods cannot
It detects only surface defects
It works on non-conductive materials only

q: What materials are used for modern aircraft brake discs?
opts: Steel only
Carbon fibre composite (CFC), which is lighter and can withstand higher temperatures than steel
Titanium alloy for its corrosion resistance
Aluminium alloy to reduce weight

q: Why must carbon disc brakes be handled carefully?
opts: They are extremely heavy and can cause injury
They are brittle and can crack if dropped or subjected to impact
They contain toxic materials that require special handling
They retain heat for a long time and can cause burns

q: What does the anti-skid system do?
opts: It applies equal braking force to all wheels simultaneously
It prevents wheel lockup by modulating hydraulic pressure if wheel speed drops below a threshold
It increases braking efficiency by applying maximum pressure at all times
It controls the extension and retraction of the landing gear

q: How is brake wear checked?
opts: By measuring the temperature of the discs after landing
By measuring against wear indicator pins or disc wear limits in the maintenance manual
By visual inspection of disc surface colour
By monitoring brake pedal travel in the cockpit

q: What happens if oil or grease contaminates carbon brake discs?
opts: The discs develop surface cracks
The disc performance degrades
The disc discolours but performance is unaffected
The disc must be replaced immediately regardless of wear level

q: What must be verified before connecting ground power to an aircraft?
opts: The ground power unit is fully fuelled
The power source is compatible with the aircraft's electrical system requirements
The engines are fully shut down and cooled
The flight crew has approved the connection

q: Why must the aircraft be bonded to earth during fuelling?
opts: To prevent electrical faults in the aircraft systems
To dissipate static charges and prevent a spark that could ignite fuel vapour
To allow the fuelling rate to be measured accurately
To ensure the fuel trucks are correctly positioned

q: What must happen after pushback before the aircraft is released to the crew?
opts: The flight crew must confirm hydraulic pressure is normal
The towbar must be disconnected and the nose wheel steering reconnected
The ground power must be disconnected and stored
The chocks must be removed and the pushback vehicle reversed

q: What could happen if the nose gear is turned beyond its stated steering angle limits during towing?
opts: The aircraft's hydraulic system could be overpressurised
The nose gear could be structurally damaged
The nose gear steering system could automatically disengage
The aircraft cannot be steered and will travel in a straight line

q: What is the maximum towing speed based on?
opts: The type of ground surface
The manufacturer's specification for the aircraft
The distance to be covered
The weight of the aircraft

q: What unit is commonly used to measure engine vibration?
opts: psi (pounds per square inch)
IPS (inches per second) or similar
RPM (revolutions per minute)
Hz (hertz)

q: What could an increase in engine vibration level indicate?
opts: Excessive fuel flow
Fan or turbine blade damage, bearing wear, or rotor imbalance
A leak in the oil system
Compressor inlet contamination

q: What maintenance may be required when vibration levels exceed alert thresholds?
opts: Immediate engine shutdown and ground test
Borescope inspection, oil sampling, or rotor trim balancing
Replacement of the engine vibration sensors
Adjustment of the engine fuel flow schedule

q: How is rotor trim balancing performed?
opts: By removing and replacing individual fan blades
By adding or removing small balance weights at designated positions on the fan or compressor rotor
By adjusting the rotor bearing clearances
By applying sealant to the fan blade attachment points

q: What is the benefit of regularly trending vibration data?
opts: It allows the pilot to monitor engine health in real time
It helps identify gradual deterioration before it reaches a critical level
It reduces the need for borescope inspections
It records vibration data for insurance purposes

q: What would happen if the aircraft's centre of gravity (CG) is outside limits?
opts: The aircraft's speed would be reduced
The aircraft could become uncontrollable or structurally unsafe
Fuel consumption would increase significantly
The pressurization system would fail

q: What is "moment" in weight and balance calculations?
opts: The distance from the reference datum to a weight item
Weight multiplied by arm
The total weight of all items on the aircraft
The time taken to load the aircraft

q: When must weight and balance calculations be completed?
opts: Only when the aircraft is fully loaded
Before every flight
Only after a major structural repair
At each scheduled maintenance check

q: What establishes the basic empty weight of an aircraft?
opts: The maximum takeoff weight certification
It is established at manufacture and confirmed after any modification or major structural repair
It is calculated before each flight based on the fuel load
It is established annually by the regulatory authority

q: Why must the maximum certificated weights never be exceeded?
opts: Exceeding them increases fuel consumption dramatically
Exceeding them can stress structural components beyond their design limits
Exceeding them voids the aircraft insurance
Exceeding them triggers automated safety systems that ground the aircraft

q: What conditions do accumulated dirt and pollutants create on aircraft skin?
opts: Conditions that promote paint adhesion
Conditions that promote corrosion, especially in dissimilar metal joints and crevice areas
Conditions that reduce aerodynamic drag
Conditions that reduce static electricity build-up

q: Where should high-pressure water jets NOT be directed during aircraft washing?
opts: At the wing leading edges
At seals, vents, pressure-sensitive instruments, or into control surface hinge gaps
At the engine nacelles
At the landing gear bays

q: What must be checked after washing the aircraft?
opts: That the aircraft weight has not changed significantly
That all drain holes are clear
That all lighting systems are operational
That all access panels are correctly closed

q: What are Corrosion Inhibiting Compounds (CICs) used for?
opts: To remove existing corrosion from metal surfaces
To provide a protective film on specified internal structural areas
To seal structural rivets against moisture ingress
To replace the function of primer on repainted surfaces

q: After finding corrosion during washing, what must be done?
opts: The area must be immediately sanded and repainted
The corrosion must be assessed against the structural repair manual to determine if it is within limits or requires treatment/repair
The aircraft must be grounded until the corrosion is removed
The corrosion must be photographed and sent to the manufacturer for disposition

q: What is the standard storage pressure for gaseous oxygen cylinders on aircraft?
opts: 500 psi
1850 psi
3000 psi
100 psi

q: How does a chemical oxygen generator produce oxygen?
opts: By electrolysis of water
By a chemical reaction initiated by a firing pin when the mask lanyard is pulled
By heating liquid oxygen to convert it to gas
By compressing ambient air and removing nitrogen

q: Why are chemical oxygen generators used in passenger overhead panels?
opts: They are cheaper than gaseous systems
They are lightweight and require no high-pressure plumbing
They produce oxygen more quickly than gaseous systems
They can supply more oxygen per unit weight than gaseous systems

q: Why must oil and grease be kept away from oxygen system components?
opts: Oil reduces the efficiency of the oxygen regulator
Oil and grease can ignite violently on contact with high-pressure oxygen
Oil contaminates the oxygen and makes it toxic
Oil reduces the pressure in the oxygen cylinders

q: In which aircraft type are liquid oxygen (LOX) systems typically used?
opts: Short-haul commuter aircraft
Military aircraft
Large commercial transport aircraft
Business jets

q: What does the Fault Isolation Manual (FIM) provide?
opts: Step-by-step maintenance procedures for scheduled tasks
Assistance to technicians in troubleshooting system faults
A breakdown of all parts by assembly and part number
Electrical circuit schematics for the aircraft systems

q: What information does the Illustrated Parts Catalogue (IPC) provide?
opts: Maintenance procedures for each system
Part identification by assembly breakdown and part number
Fault codes and their remedies
Wiring diagrams for each system

q: What must a technician do upon completing a task card?
opts: Return the task card to the planning department for filing
Sign the task card to certify the work was done in accordance with the referenced documentation
Pass the task card to the quality inspector for verification
Submit the task card to the aircraft commander

q: For how long must completed task cards be retained?
opts: For one year after the maintenance check
For the required regulatory period
Until the next scheduled maintenance check
Until the aircraft is retired

q: What is the advantage of Electronic Maintenance Documentation (EMD) systems?
opts: They reduce the number of maintenance tasks required
They improve traceability and reduce paper use
They allow maintenance to be performed without reference documents
They automatically certify completed maintenance tasks

q: What are downlocks in the landing gear system?
opts: Locks that prevent the gear from extending in flight
Mechanical devices that prevent the gear from collapsing when extended under load
Hydraulic locks that hold the gear in the retracted position
Locks activated by the cockpit gear handle

q: What happens during the normal landing gear retraction sequence?
opts: Gear retracts first, then doors open
Gear doors open, gear retracts, gear doors close
Gear retracts and doors remain open until the next extension
Gear doors close when gear is up, then doors open again

q: What methods can be used for emergency landing gear extension?
opts: Manual mechanical extension by the flight crew
Alternative hydraulic source, pneumatic pressure, or gravity (free-fall)
Engine bleed air pressure
Battery-powered electric actuators only

q: What safety conditions must be met before a landing gear retraction test on the ground?
opts: The aircraft engines must be running
The aircraft must be on jacks and the wheel well area clear of personnel
The aircraft hydraulic pressure must be at maximum
The flight crew must be in the cockpit for the test

q: How is landing gear position indicated in the cockpit?
opts: By mechanical flags visible through the cockpit windows
By proximity switches or microswitches signalling the cockpit indicator lights
By pressure sensors in the actuators
By the weight-on-wheels switch only

q: What type of corrosion occurs between two dissimilar metals in electrical contact?
opts: Pitting corrosion
Galvanic corrosion
Intergranular corrosion
Fretting corrosion

q: What is intergranular corrosion?
opts: Corrosion that forms small deep holes on the surface
Corrosion along the grain boundaries of an alloy that can seriously weaken structure while being invisible from the surface
Corrosion appearing as a network of worm-like threads under paint
Corrosion resulting from micro-motion between parts

q: What type of corrosion results from micro-motion between closely fitted parts?
opts: Galvanic corrosion
Fretting corrosion
Filiform corrosion
Exfoliation corrosion

q: How is corrosion treated after removal?
opts: By applying paint directly over the bare metal
By assessing against corrosion limits, applying conversion coating, primer, and topcoat
By welding over the corroded area
By applying a corrosion inhibiting compound only

q: What distinguishes exfoliation corrosion?
opts: It forms small pits on the metal surface
It is a type of intergranular corrosion that causes layers of the material to peel away
It occurs preferentially in weld zones
It occurs only on the outer skin of the aircraft

q: What technology do most modern fuel quantity indicating systems use?
opts: Float-type mechanical gauges
Capacitance probes that measure changes in electrical capacitance as fuel level changes
Ultrasonic sensors that measure the fuel surface level
Weight sensors mounted under the fuel tanks

q: Why is the fuel quantity reading temperature-compensated?
opts: Because fuel temperature affects aircraft performance
Because fuel density changes with temperature, affecting the capacitance calculation
Because capacitance probes are sensitive to temperature damage
Because the cockpit display would freeze in cold temperatures

q: What is the purpose of the totaliser in the fuel quantity system?
opts: To measure the fuel flow rate to each engine
To show the total fuel quantity across all tanks
To calculate the fuel burn rate
To provide a warning when fuel is low

q: What maintenance tasks are involved in the FQIS?
opts: Regular replacement of the capacitance probes
Probe resistance checks, leak checks at connections, and functional tests to verify calibration
Visual inspection of the tank interior through probe access holes
Cleaning of probe surfaces and repainting

q: What could cause a fuel quantity probe to read out of range?
opts: Excessive vibration in the wing structure
A faulty probe or fuel contamination affecting it
An oil leak into the fuel tank
Incorrect fuel type loaded into the tank

q: How is cabin pressure controlled during flight?
opts: By varying the engine bleed air supply only
By controlling the outflow valves to regulate the rate at which air is vented overboard
By adjusting the air conditioning packs
By modulating the aircraft's climb and descent rate

q: What is the function of the safety relief valve in the pressurization system?
opts: To maintain the minimum cabin pressure during descent
To protect the fuselage from overpressurisation by opening if differential pressure exceeds the maximum design limit
To provide emergency oxygen in case of pressurization loss
To supply fresh outside air when the bleed system fails

q: What does the negative pressure relief valve prevent?
opts: The cabin from depressurising too rapidly
Outside air pressure from exceeding cabin pressure during descent
The fuselage from exceeding maximum positive pressure differential
The bleed air supply from exceeding maximum pressure

q: What maintenance task verifies the structural integrity of the fuselage under pressurization loading?
opts: A functional test of the outflow valve
A pressure differential test of the aircraft fuselage structure
An inspection of the door seals
A check of the cabin pressure controller setting

q: At what thresholds must cabin altitude and differential pressure warnings alert the crew?
opts: At 8,000 ft cabin altitude only
At the correct thresholds specified in the maintenance manual, verified by functional testing
At any cabin altitude above sea level
At the pilot's discretion based on the flight conditions

q: What inspection is required after a turbine engine overtemperature event?
opts: A magnetic particle inspection of the engine casing
A borescope inspection of the combustion chamber, turbine, and nozzle guide vanes
An oil sample analysis
A visual inspection of the external engine case

q: What must happen if turbine damage exceeds defined limits after an exceedance?
opts: The engine may continue operating with enhanced monitoring
The engine must be removed and sent to an overhaul facility
The engine's temperature limit is recertified at a higher level
The aircraft is ferried to a maintenance base with the engine at reduced power

q: What risk does an overspeed event pose to the engine?
opts: Damage to the fuel nozzles
Plastic deformation of rotating components such as fan, compressor, and turbine discs
Overheating of the oil system
Seizure of the engine starter

q: What information must be recorded in the engine logbook after an exceedance?
opts: The technician's opinion on the severity of the event
The specific parameter exceeded, duration, and conditions
The engine's remaining service life
A list of all components likely to be damaged

q: Why must exceedances be reported to the engine manufacturer?
opts: For warranty claim purposes
For review and fleet trending
To receive replacement parts immediately
To update the engine's type certificate data

q: What must be included in a maintenance record entry?
opts: The engineer's personal opinion on the aircraft condition
Date, work description, documentation reference, part numbers, serial numbers, and certifying engineer signature and licence number
The aircraft's next scheduled maintenance date
The cost of the maintenance carried out

q: Why must the maintenance history accompany a life-limited component when transferred to another aircraft?
opts: To allow the receiving operator to recertify the component
To maintain traceability of the component's operating life
To reduce the time required to install the component
To comply with the manufacturer's export requirements

q: What are the consequences of falsifying maintenance records?
opts: A written warning from the maintenance organisation
Revocation of licences and possible criminal prosecution
Mandatory retraining of the engineer involved
A financial penalty only

q: How must maintenance records be kept?
opts: Only in electronic form for easy access
Available and legible, accessible to regulatory authorities at any time
In a secure location accessible only to senior management
Retained in the aircraft at all times

q: What advantage do electronic maintenance record systems offer?
opts: They replace the need for certifying engineers
They improve accessibility and auditability
They eliminate the need for regulatory oversight
They automatically generate maintenance plans

q: What are the three main types of hydraulic filters by location?
opts: Main filter, standby filter, and emergency filter
Pressure filters, return filters, and case drain filters
In-line filters, reservoir filters, and actuator filters
High-pressure filters, medium-pressure filters, and low-pressure filters

q: How are hydraulic filter elements rated?
opts: By their flow rate in gallons per minute
By the size of particles they capture (e.g., 3 micron or 10 micron)
By their operating pressure rating in psi
By their material composition

q: What indicates that a hydraulic filter element needs replacement?
opts: A change in hydraulic fluid colour
A filter condition indicator (bypass or differential pressure indicator) showing clogging
A drop in hydraulic system pressure
An increase in hydraulic fluid temperature

q: What should be done if excessive metallic particles are found in a replaced filter element?
opts: The filter element is replaced more frequently
A system investigation should be initiated to identify the source of the metal particles
The hydraulic fluid is drained and replaced
The metallic particles are noted and the system returned to service

q: What must be checked after replacing a hydraulic filter?
opts: That the hydraulic fluid level in the reservoir is correct
That the system operates correctly and the filter area is leak-free
That all hydraulic actuators function correctly
That the filter bypass indicator has reset

q: What happens when a fire is detected in an engine nacelle?
opts: The engine automatically shuts down
A warning light and aural alert are triggered in the cockpit
The extinguisher bottle discharges automatically
The hydraulic system is isolated immediately

q: What does pressing the engine fire push button do?
opts: Discharges the fire extinguisher into the nacelle immediately
Isolates the engine from fuel, hydraulics, and electrical systems, and arms the fire extinguisher
Sends an emergency signal to ATC
Activates the APU to take over engine functions

q: Why do large aircraft have two extinguisher bottles per engine?
opts: One bottle cools the engine while the other suppresses the fire
To provide a second shot if the first does not extinguish the fire
One bottle is for engine fires and one for nacelle structure fires
To comply with CAA regulations for twin-engine aircraft

q: How does cargo compartment fire suppression work?
opts: Using the same high-concentration agent as engine nacelle suppression
Using a lower concentration agent delivered over time to deplete oxygen levels
Using water mist from a dedicated cargo wash system
Using halon gas released as a single burst

q: How is fire extinguisher bottle serviceability verified before every flight?
opts: By weighing the bottle
By checking the pressure gauge on the bottle
By activating a test circuit
By visual inspection of the discharge indicator

q: What damage types can affect composite structures?
opts: Rust, pitting, and intergranular corrosion
Surface scratches, dents, delamination, disbonds, and through-cracks
Fatigue cracks, stress corrosion, and galvanic corrosion
Wear, erosion, and surface oxidation

q: How is tap testing used on composite structures?
opts: A metal pick is used to probe for cracks in the surface
The structure is tapped and a dull or hollow sound indicates delamination beneath
A calibrated instrument measures surface hardness to detect damage
The structure is struck with a hammer and the vibration frequency is measured

q: Why must composite structures be dried before repair?
opts: Moisture causes the surface to become porous, preventing adhesive bonding
Moisture absorption can weaken the material and affect the quality of bonded repairs
Moisture can react with the repair resin and create toxic vapours
Moisture increases the weight of the repair patch beyond allowable limits

q: What is used to map the extent of delamination within a composite structure more accurately than tap testing?
opts: X-ray inspection
Ultrasonic inspection
Eddy current testing
Dye penetrant inspection

q: What must composite repairs comply with?
opts: Any repair method approved by the maintenance engineer
Approved repair schemes from the SRM or manufacturer-approved data
Military standards for composite repair
Industry standard guidelines published by the composite repair industry

q: What is the purpose of the fuel boost pump?
opts: To increase fuel pressure for delivery to the passengers
To pressurise fuel for transfer and ensure positive pressure at the HP pump inlet to prevent cavitation
To regulate fuel flow to each individual combustion chamber
To provide emergency fuel supply if the main pump fails

q: What is the function of the fuel heater?
opts: To heat the fuel to improve combustion efficiency
To prevent ice crystal formation in the fuel at high altitude
To reduce fuel viscosity at low temperatures
To warm the fuel before it enters the fuel metering unit

q: What does the Fuel Metering Unit (FMU) control?
opts: The fuel quantity in the wing tanks
Fuel flow to the combustion chamber in response to power lever position and engine parameters
The pressure of the fuel delivered to each nozzle
The temperature of the fuel entering the combustion section

q: What is the function of the fuel nozzles?
opts: To regulate the flow of fuel to the combustion chamber
To atomise the fuel to promote efficient combustion
To filter the fuel before combustion
To cool the combustion chamber walls with fuel

q: What maintenance is performed on fuel system components?
opts: Only replacement at fixed intervals regardless of condition
Regular inspection, cleaning, and testing at specified intervals in the CMM
Visual inspection only during A-checks
Complete replacement at each C-check

q: What are the two main battery types used in commercial aviation?
opts: Lead-acid and lithium-ion
Nickel-cadmium (NiCd) and valve-regulated lead-acid (VRLA)
Nickel-metal hydride and lithium polymer
Silver-zinc and nickel-cadmium

q: What is a battery capacity check?
opts: A visual inspection of the battery case and terminals
A discharge test performed at specified intervals to ensure the battery can deliver its rated amp-hours
A measurement of the battery's open-circuit voltage
A check of the electrolyte specific gravity only

q: Why must battery charging be carried out in a well-ventilated area?
opts: The charging process generates heat that must be dissipated
Gases are released during charging that could accumulate and become a fire or explosion hazard
Charging in confined spaces can cause electromagnetic interference with avionics
Regulations require ventilation to prevent damage to the charging equipment

q: What must be done with electrolyte spills?
opts: Cleaned with dry cloths immediately
Neutralised with the appropriate solution before cleaning
Left to evaporate naturally in a ventilated area
Reported to the regulatory authority

q: What indicates that a battery should be removed from service?
opts: The battery case shows minor surface corrosion
The battery cannot meet its rated capacity requirement during a discharge test
The battery terminals require retorquing
The battery electrolyte requires topping up

q: Why are aircraft tyres inflated with nitrogen instead of compressed air?
opts: Nitrogen provides better cushioning at high landing speeds
Nitrogen is dry, preventing moisture-related corrosion, and does not support combustion
Nitrogen provides a more stable pressure across temperature changes than air
Nitrogen reduces the weight of the inflated tyre

q: Why must tyre pressure be checked with the aircraft on jacks or the tyre off the aircraft?
opts: The aircraft's weight makes it unsafe to measure pressure when on the ground
The loaded condition changes the measured pressure
The tyre pressure gauge cannot be connected when the aircraft is on the ground
Nitrogen pressure is affected by the aircraft's weight on the tyre

q: What is the purpose of heat-sensitive fusible plugs in aircraft wheels?

To release if brakes overheat, preventing tyre blowout by releasing pressure
To seal the wheel against nitrogen leakage
To prevent the tyre from deflating if the bead is damaged

q: How is tyre service life measured?
opts: In flight hours and calendar months only
In landings (cycles) and calendar time
In miles or kilometres of ground rolling
In number of brake applications

q: Which of the following is a type of tyre damage assessed against allowable limits?
opts: Nitrogen oxidation within the tyre
Cuts, abrasions, flat spots from locked-wheel skids, and sidewall damage
Internal delamination of the tyre body
Chemical contamination of the bead wire

q: Where is the APU typically located on a commercial aircraft?
opts: In the nose cone
In the tail cone
In the main gear fairing
In the centre wing section

q: What does the APU provide during ground operations?
opts: Hydraulic pressure for towing only
Electrical power and bleed air for engine starting and air conditioning
Oxygen for crew and passengers
Fuel transfer to the wing tanks

q: What conditions must be confirmed before starting the APU?
opts: Main engines must be shut down and cooled
Both the fire detection and suppression systems must be confirmed serviceable
The aircraft must be connected to ground power

q: What is borescope inspection of the APU turbine performed to detect?

Blade erosion, cracks, and other turbine section defects
Contamination in the APU air intake

q: How are APU hours and cycles tracked?
opts: Combined with the main engine hours and cycles
Separately from the main engines, with their own overhaul and life-limited part requirements
By the aircraft's main maintenance computer system
Only by calendar time intervals

q: What must be done before aircraft towing begins?
opts: The pilot must be informed and seated in the cockpit
Chocks removed, gear pins removed, all doors secured, and communication plan established
The APU must be running to provide hydraulic power for steering
Ground power must be connected to the aircraft

q: What is the risk if the nose gear approaches its maximum steering angle during towing?
opts: The towbar will disengage from the nose gear
Structural damage to the nose gear or the aircraft
The tow truck will lose steering control
The aircraft's wheel brakes will automatically apply

q: What must be done after pushback is completed?
opts: The chocks must be inserted before the towbar is disconnected
The nose gear steering pin must be removed and nose gear steering system reconnected
The aircraft must be connected to external electrical power
The pilot must confirm the aircraft is ready for departure

q: What is the role of the wing walker during towing?
opts: To control the speed of the tow tractor
To confirm clearance on each side of the aircraft
To operate the aircraft's wing flaps
To maintain communication with the control tower

q: Why must the correct towbar type be used for each aircraft and nose gear combination?
opts: Different towbars have different maximum towing speeds
Using the wrong towbar can damage the nose gear attachment points or exceed structural limits
Only the correct towbar will connect to the nose gear
Incorrect towbars increase the risk of static electricity buildup

q: What provides the initial rotational force for starting most large turbofan engines?
opts: An electric starter motor
An air turbine starter (ATS) driven by bleed air
A hydraulic starter using engine oil pressure
A pneumatic motor connected to the ground power unit

q: How does the Air Turbine Starter disengage after engine start?
opts: The flight crew manually disengages it from the cockpit
It disengages automatically through a sprag clutch when the engine reaches self-sustaining speed
It is disengaged by closing the bleed air supply valve
It disengages when the engine reaches idle speed

q: What is the starter duty cycle?
opts: The total number of starts allowed before overhaul
The allowed number of consecutive start attempts before a cooling period is required
The time from start initiation to idle speed
The maximum bleed air pressure allowed for starting

q: What should be done if an engine fails to light off within the specified time?
opts: Continue cranking until the engine fires
Abort the start and motor the engine without ignition to purge unburned fuel
Increase the bleed air pressure and try again immediately
Switch to the electric starter as a backup

q: What happens if a start is attempted without clearing unburned fuel from the previous failed start?
opts: The engine fails to rotate
A hot start may occur due to ignition of pooled unburned fuel
The fuel system automatically purges itself
The fuel nozzles become blocked

q: What is inspected on the aircraft skin during maintenance checks?
opts: Weight and material composition
Dents, scratches, cracks, corrosion, missing fasteners, and evidence of fluid leaks
Paint colour consistency and aerodynamic finish
Electromagnetic shielding properties

q: When can a dent in a non-critical area be left in place?
opts: When it is confirmed that no aerodynamic effect is caused
When it does not exceed the allowable depth and does not include cracks
When the pilot has confirmed it is acceptable
When it is more than 24 hours old and has not grown

q: What type of dent must always be repaired?
opts: Any dent visible from more than 5 meters
Dents with cracks or sharp radii
Any dent deeper than 1mm
Dents in high-visibility areas

q: What is the reference document for dent and scratch allowable damage limits?
opts: The Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM)
The Structural Repair Manual (SRM)
The Component Maintenance Manual (CMM)
The Illustrated Parts Catalogue (IPC)

q: What must be done when fluid weeping is found through the skin or around panels?
opts: Clean the area and monitor for recurrence
Investigate and identify the source of the fluid
Seal with sealant as a temporary repair
Record in the technical log and defer to the next scheduled check

q: What is required before entering an aircraft fuel tank?
opts: Engine shutdown and fuel drain only
A confined space risk assessment, permit to enter, atmospheric monitoring, and safety attendant
Fire extinguisher on standby and ventilation only
Supervisor approval and personal protective equipment only

q: What type of tools must be used inside fuel tanks?
opts: Standard calibrated tools
Approved non-sparking type tools
Electrically insulated tools
Lightweight composite-handled tools

q: What electrical equipment is permitted inside fuel tanks?
opts: Standard battery-operated tools
Intrinsically safe (IS) or explosion-proof equipment
110V power tools with residual current device protection
Equipment rated for use in wet environments

q: Why must workers not wear coveralls with metal zips or buttons inside fuel tanks?
opts: Metal zips cause contamination of the fuel
Metal can cause sparks that could ignite fuel vapour
Metal zips are prohibited by fire regulations in all confined spaces
Metal objects increase the risk of electrostatic discharge from the worker's body

q: Why should no person work alone inside a fuel tank?
opts: Two people are required to complete the work efficiently
Due to the confined space and vapour hazard, a person incapacitated inside needs to be rescued by a safety attendant
The permit requires two signatures from inside workers
Two workers are needed to operate the required monitoring equipment

q: What is the purpose of a maintenance task card?
opts: To record the aircraft's flight history
To define each individual maintenance task and control its execution
To document the cost of maintenance operations
To record the qualifications of the maintenance team

q: What must a technician do if a step on a task card cannot be completed as written?
opts: Improvise an alternative method to complete the task
Raise the issue with the maintenance supervisor before proceeding
Skip the step and note it in the comments section
Complete the adjacent steps and return to the problem step later

q: After completing a task card, how is it used as a maintenance record?
opts: It is returned to the planning department for revision
It is filed as part of the maintenance record
It is discarded after the maintenance check is closed out
It is forwarded to the regulatory authority

q: What happens when a certifying engineer signs a task card?
opts: They confirm that the aircraft is ready for flight
They certify that the work was done in accordance with the referenced documentation
They authorise the next maintenance task to begin
They approve the materials used for the task

q: If a technician cannot complete a step as written, what MUST NOT happen?
opts: The supervisor must be called to assist
The technician must not improvise and proceed without approval
The step must be crossed out and rewritten
The task card must be returned to the planning team

q: What does Electrical Load Analysis (ELA) verify?
opts: That all circuit breakers are rated correctly
That total electrical demand does not exceed available supply under all operating conditions
That all wiring is correctly identified and labelled
That the battery system can sustain all loads during engine failure

q: What must be done when a modification adds new electrical consumers?
opts: Additional generators must be installed
The ELA must be updated to verify that system capacity is not exceeded
The modification must be tested in flight only
The circuit breaker panel must be replaced

q: What does excessive voltage drop in a circuit indicate?
opts: The circuit breaker is too small for the load
Overloaded or faulty wiring
The generator is producing too much power
The bus bar has been incorrectly installed

q: What must be used for aircraft wiring repairs?
opts: Any electrical tape that can withstand the voltage
Approved splicing techniques and materials specified in the WDM and AMM
Standard automotive wiring connectors
Heat-shrink tubing and standard solder

q: What does a wiring inspection look for?
opts: Correct wire colour coding only
Chafing, broken insulation, loose connections, and evidence of overheating
Correct routing as shown in the wiring diagram
Secure attachment of wire bundles to the structure

q: When is a test flight typically required after maintenance?
opts: After every maintenance check, regardless of scope
As specified in the AMM or by the certifying engineer based on the nature of the work
Only when the regulatory authority requires it
After any component is replaced on the aircraft

q: Which of the following maintenance activities would most likely require a test flight?
opts: Routine tyre pressure check
Engine change or flight control rigging adjustment
Aircraft washing and corrosion treatment
Replacement of a landing light bulb

q: What must be completed before a test flight?
opts: All passengers must be disembarked and cargo offloaded
A comprehensive pre-flight inspection and closure of all maintenance documentation
A fuel system pressure check and engine power assurance run
Completion of an air traffic control flight plan only

q: What must happen to discrepancies found during a test flight?
opts: They are noted and presented to the customer at delivery
They must be recorded and rectified before the aircraft is released to normal service
They are deferred under the MEL for the first revenue service
Minor discrepancies may be cleared by the test pilot in the aircraft log

q: Who may perform a maintenance test flight?
opts: Any qualified commercial pilot
Qualified test pilots or pilots with the appropriate authorisation for such flights
The certifying engineer if they hold a pilot's licence
Any pilot who has operated the aircraft type before

q: What must an aircraft skin repair restore?
opts: Only the structural strength of the original skin
Original strength, stiffness, and aerodynamic profile
The original skin material and colour
The original fastener pattern

q: What is a "flush doubler" repair?
opts: A repair using two layers of skin panels riveted together
A repair patch installed flush with the surface using countersunk fasteners to maintain the aerodynamic profile
A repair that replaces the damaged panel completely
A repair that adds a patch on both sides of the damage

q: What determines the correct repair patch specification?
opts: The technician's experience with similar repairs
The SRM, which specifies the required material, thickness, and temper
The nearest available compatible material in stores
The aircraft manufacturer's helpline

q: What is checked after riveted fastener installation in a skin repair?
opts: That the rivet pattern matches the original design
Correct driven head dimensions and edge distance
That the surface is flat and smooth
That the repair matches the surrounding paint colour

q: Where are aircraft skin repairs documented?
opts: In the maintenance manual
In the structural repair log
In the technical log
In the task card only

q: What must be ensured when loading cargo into an aircraft?
opts: That all cargo is the same weight to ensure balance
That the aircraft remains within weight and balance limits and cargo cannot shift during flight
That cargo is placed as far aft as possible to improve CG
That all cargo is loaded at the same time to save time

q: What is a "Unit Load Device" (ULD)?
opts: A device that measures the weight of cargo before loading
A container or pallet that is locked into the aircraft floor with latches
A restraint system for oversized cargo
A loading tool used to move heavy cargo

q: What regulations govern the loading of dangerous goods?
opts: ICAO Annex 6 — Operation of Aircraft
IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations
CAA Air Navigation Order
FAA Federal Aviation Regulations Part 121

q: What structural damage can result from overloaded cargo?
opts: Damage to the main wing structure
Structural damage to the cargo floor
Damage to the fuselage frames
Damage to the overhead luggage compartments

q: Why can undeclared dangerous goods in cargo pose a serious safety risk?
opts: They increase the aircraft's weight beyond allowable limits
They may not be handled correctly, packaged appropriately, or placed in the correct location, leading to fire, explosion, or toxic exposure
They prevent correct weight and balance calculations
They may contaminate other cargo

q: What is the primary source of hydraulic pressure on most aircraft?
opts: Electric motor-driven pumps
Engine-driven pumps (EDPs) driven from the engine accessory gearbox
Power transfer units (PTUs)
Ram air turbine pumps

q: What does a Power Transfer Unit (PTU) do?
opts: Transfers hydraulic fluid from one system to another
Transfers hydraulic power from one system to another using a motor-pump combination, without transferring fluid
Transfers electrical power to operate hydraulic pumps
Transfers hydraulic pressure to the flight controls only

q: When is an electric motor-driven pump (EMDP) typically used?
opts: As the primary pump at all times
As a backup or during ground operations when engines are not running
Only in emergency conditions when the EDP fails
Only during high-demand operations such as landing gear retraction

q: What does engine-driven pump maintenance include?
opts: Replacement of the pump drive shaft at each A-check
Checking for external leaks, monitoring case drain pressure and temperature, and inspecting the drive shaft seal
Replacement of the pump filter element only
Pressure testing the pump against a calibrated flow bench

q: How is pump serviceability verified during maintenance?
opts: By checking the hydraulic fluid colour
By testing pump output pressure and flow against maintenance manual limits
By monitoring the cockpit hydraulic pressure gauge
By running the engine to maximum power and checking for pressure drops

q: How are gaseous oxygen cylinders tested for integrity?
opts: By X-ray inspection of the cylinder wall
By visual inspection and hydrostatic testing at specified intervals
By measuring the wall thickness with ultrasound
By conducting a pressure decay test using nitrogen

q: Why must tools and personnel hands be clean when working on oxygen systems?
opts: To prevent contamination that could reduce oxygen purity
Oil and grease can ignite violently in high-pressure oxygen
Grease can cause corrosion in oxygen system plumbing
Oil contamination clogs the oxygen regulators

q: What is the difference between continuous flow and pressure demand oxygen masks?
opts: Continuous flow masks are for crew use, pressure demand for passengers
Continuous flow masks deliver a constant flow, while pressure demand masks are for crew at high altitude delivering oxygen on demand
Continuous flow masks operate on positive pressure, demand masks on negative pressure
There is no functional difference, only a physical difference

q: When must oxygen system connections be leak-tested?
opts: Only when a leak is suspected
After any connection is opened
At each annual inspection
When the oxygen cylinder is replaced only

q: What is the minimum pressure requirement for oxygen cylinders?
opts: They may be used at any pressure above atmospheric
They must be above the minimum specified pressure; below this they are recharged or replaced
They must be at maximum fill pressure before each flight
Cylinder pressure is not a maintenance concern

q: What is the basis for developing an aircraft maintenance programme (AMP)?
opts: Manufacturer's recommendations, ADs, SBs, and operational experience
The operator's commercial preferences and budget constraints
Regulatory authority requirements only
Industry average maintenance intervals

q: How are maintenance intervals typically expressed in an AMP?
opts: Only in calendar time
In flight hours, cycles, and calendar time
Only in flight hours
In aircraft miles flown

q: What is "escalation" in the context of maintenance intervals?
opts: Increasing the complexity of maintenance tasks
Extending an interval beyond the standard value, based on operating experience
Upgrading the maintenance standard to a higher level
Moving a maintenance task to a heavier check

q: What could happen if an operator fails to comply with the AMP?
opts: The operator receives a written warning only
The aircraft may lose its airworthiness status
The operator's insurance becomes invalid
The manufacturer withdraws technical support

q: Who audits AMP compliance?
opts: The manufacturer's service representative only
The quality assurance function and the regulatory authority
The airline's finance department
The flight crew before each flight

q: Where do bacteria and fungi survive in an aircraft fuel tank?
opts: Throughout the bulk fuel mass
At the fuel-water interface at the bottom of the tank
On the upper walls of the tank near the fuel vents
In the fuel transfer lines only

q: What is Microbial Induced Corrosion (MIC)?
opts: Corrosion caused by dissimilar metal contact in the tank
Corrosion caused by acids produced by bacteria and fungi as metabolic byproducts
Corrosion caused by water contamination in the fuel
Corrosion caused by incorrect fuel type being loaded

q: How is MIC treated in fuel tanks?
opts: By installing a nitrogen inert system
By draining, cleaning, applying biocide, and inspecting for structural damage
By adding anti-freeze to the fuel
By sealing the fuel tank completely to prevent further moisture ingress

q: When pitting corrosion is found in a fuel tank, what is the next step?
opts: Seal the pitted area with fuel tank sealant
Measure the pitting depth and assess against allowable limits in the SRM
Replace the panel without further assessment
Apply anti-corrosion primer directly over the pitting

q: How do corrosion inhibitors added to fuel help?
opts: They prevent water from entering the tank
They reduce the rate of corrosion on the tank surfaces
They kill bacteria and fungi in the fuel
They prevent sediment from settling at the bottom of the tank

q: How are pilot inputs transmitted in a mechanical flight control system?
opts: Through hydraulic actuators only
Through cables, pulleys, and rods to move the control surfaces
Through electrical wiring to flight control computers
Through a combination of hydraulic and pneumatic actuators

q: What is envelope protection in a fly-by-wire system?
opts: A physical barrier preventing flight crew from over-controlling
A function that prevents the pilot from commanding movements that exceed structural or aerodynamic limits
An emergency system that limits control surface travel in turbulence
A software protection that records all control inputs

q: What maintenance is performed on fly-by-wire systems?
opts: Regular cable tension checks and pulley lubrication
Functional testing of sensors, computers, and actuators, and software version verification
Rigging adjustments to maintain correct neutral positions
Inspection of control surface hinge bushings

q: What must be performed after any work on a flight control system?
opts: A pressure test of the hydraulic actuators
A full range of movement check to verify correct operation before return to service
A functional test of the autopilot system
An aerodynamic assessment of the control surface profile

q: What does a rigging check of mechanical flight controls verify?
opts: That the hydraulic actuators are correctly installed
That correct control surface positions and movement ranges are maintained
That the control surface materials meet specification
That the fly-by-wire computers are correctly interfaced with the mechanical backup

q: What is the primary source of pneumatic pressure on most large jet aircraft?
opts: Dedicated compressors driven by the APU
Bleed air from the engine compressor stages
High-pressure hydraulic air reservoirs
Ground air supply units

q: Why are precoolers used in the bleed air system?
opts: To increase the pressure of bleed air before it enters the cabin
To reduce the temperature of bleed air before it enters the pneumatic manifold
To filter contamination from the bleed air supply
To separate moisture from the bleed air

q: What does the overpressure protection system do in the pneumatic system?
opts: It increases pressure to maintain supply during high demand
It closes supply valves if bleed air pressure exceeds limits to protect the distribution system
It redirects excess pressure to the cabin pressurization system
It activates the APU to provide backup bleed air

q: What damage can hot air leaks in the bleed air system cause?
opts: Contamination of the fuel system
Significant structural damage if undetected
Overpressurisation of the aircraft cabin
Loss of thrust on the affected engine

q: What does maintenance of the pneumatic system include?
opts: Replacement of all bleed air valves at fixed intervals
Inspection of ducts for hot air leaks, testing of sensors and PRSOVs, and checking duct couplings
Calibration of the cockpit pressure gauges
Replacement of the engine bleed air manifold at each C-check

q: What are the four pillars of an SMS?
opts: Quality, safety, training, and documentation
Safety policy, safety risk management, safety assurance, and safety promotion
Hazard identification, risk assessment, risk control, and safety monitoring
Procedures, documentation, auditing, and training

q: What is the role of safety assurance in SMS?
opts: To identify new hazards in the organisation
To monitor and measure safety performance to ensure controls remain effective
To train personnel in safety procedures
To investigate accidents and incidents

q: What is essential for an effective SMS in a maintenance organisation?
opts: A strict punitive culture for all errors
An open reporting culture where personnel report errors without fear of punishment
A separate safety team independent of the maintenance organisation
A computerised SMS software system

q: What is the role of hazard reports submitted by maintenance personnel?
opts: They are reviewed and archived for regulatory compliance
They are a key input to the SMS hazard identification process
They are used only to discipline the personnel who reported the hazard
They are forwarded to the aircraft manufacturer for information

q: Why must safety be prioritised over production pressures?
opts: Regulations require safety to always take priority
Production pressures are a known contributor to maintenance errors and safety compromises
Safety is always more cost-effective in the long run
Production schedules are the responsibility of the operations department, not maintenance

q: What is the main difference between Jet A and Jet A-1?
opts: Jet A-1 is designed for military aircraft, Jet A for civilian
Jet A-1 has a lower freeze point than Jet A, making it preferred for long-range international operations
Jet A has higher energy density than Jet A-1
Jet A-1 contains biocide additives; Jet A does not

q: What is AVGAS used for?
opts: Turboprop aircraft
Piston-engine aircraft
Business jet aircraft
Turbofan engines at low altitudes

q: Why is misfuelling extremely dangerous?
opts: AVGAS is more expensive than jet fuel
Incorrect fuel types can cause engine damage or failure and may cause fire
Misfuelling causes the fuel quantity gauges to read incorrectly
Different fuel types have different tank compatibility

q: What must be checked before fuelling an aircraft?
opts: That the fuel bowser is connected to earth
The fuel type, batch number, and quality documentation
That the aircraft engines are shut down
The fuel tank capacity remaining

q: Where must fuel uplifted be recorded?
opts: In the fuel operator's daily log
In the aircraft technical log
In the loadsheet only
In the fuel bowser's calibration record
